sentences.text: Yeah, Brzezinka, [?is?]...
In Brzezinka, the Germans were trying to prove the world that we were treated so wonderfully.
So once in a while, maybe every-- not maybe-- every two weeks, they put on a entertainment.
They emptied one of the barracks a little bit.
And they put chairs there, and they put a stage.
And they brought in some very prominent-- I didn't know-- I don't know their names-- some very talented people.
Some of them were singers.
Some of them were musicians and they put on a beautiful show.
And while they were putting on the show, we have to applaud, naturally.
We had to smile.
And we had to say, yay, it's wonderful.
They were perform-- we had performers.
They were fabulous.
We had some-- some singers.
They were just-- I'm sure that they were in-- in the most wonderful back home.
And they were very prominent singers and musicians.
They had some violin players.
They were absolutely fabulous.
So they were entertaining us for about an hour.
And while they were entertaining us, they were taping us.
And they told us they're going to show everybody what a wonderful time we have in Auschwitz, that not all that they hear is true.
But what about-- but weren't your-- your heads shaved?
Yes.
Oh, definitely.
Well, sure, they have probably-- whatever they told them, they must have give them a pretty good reason why our heads were shaved, probably for-- for cleanliness, none-- none other than that.
That was the main reason, probably.
I mean, I-- I don't know.
But that's what it was.
And this happened many times over.
And every time they said, all right, here are the musicians.
Now, you're going to be entertained again.
Now, remember, we want a very pretty smile on everybody's face, which we had to do it.
Because if, I'm sure, if we wouldn't have done it, then the consequences would have been not very-- very nice.
Also, there was one day, it was at night.
And we heard a lot of crying.
And I happened to go to the door, which I wasn't supposed to.
But it was dark, and I-- I snuck to the door.
And it had a small window, and I looked through.
And I saw transports coming in by the bus loads.
And they were all gypsies.
Now, there were young people.
There were old people, their babies.
There were all kinds.
They were not spared at all, because whoever was on that-- on-- on that truck, went straight into the gas chamber.
We never saw them again.
And this happened over and over again.
They were-- there was a transport of young people.
I don't know where they came from.
They were already in a camp.
There was one camp with young people.
There was one day when we saw them actually.
We were outside because it was like [GERMAN] appell time.
And the trucks were coming by because it was in-- within a distance where we could see, because they-- actually, the crematorium was not too far from us.
It was like practically in our backyard.
And we saw these trucks coming.
And I looked over.
And there were young children, young, probably very healthy, beautiful children.
They were taken into the gas chamber and gassed, innocent children who did absolutely nothing to deserve it.
And this went on for a long time, different, from different parts of the camp.
They were always coming in with-- with truckloads of people, sick people, healthy people.
They didn't like the looks of them.
They just took them out.
That's how Mengele selected their people, because that's what they did.
If there was a blemish on somebody's body, and many times we were there naked.
He wanted to see us naked to make sure that, you know, there are-- there are no-- there's not going to be any-- any sickness.
And if he found any blemish on anybody's body, he took them out.
And that was the end of them.
Like in the-- in a-- in a shower, there was very, very degrading.
When you took a shower, the German soldiers were-- the SS were there, right there looking at us.
And they had a, like a stick.
They all had one stick, like, you know?
And they were walking around.
And they were touching everybody while they were taking a shower.
And this went on and on, every single day, day after day.
This what they were doing.
That's how they got their-- their-- their pleasures out of it.
Did-- did any of this affect your belief in God at all or--
Yes.
Yes.
We were questioning God.
We were-- my mother questioned Him more than I did.
She says, how can God watch this?
How-- how-- how can he let this happen?
Because the very first thing-- I'm going back when it started-- the very first thing we did, we saw when we went into the ghetto-- I'm going back just a-- for a few seconds.
There was a very, very religious man that my mother knew very, very well, who stood there.
And the soldiers, they-- that wasn't even the SS.
It was the Wehrmacht, they had a pair of scissors, And they were cutting his beard.
And they were cutting his-- his hair.
But-- and this is what, you know, this is what happened.
And it was very, very degrading.
And they did a lot of degrading things.
And my mother says, so where is God?
Where is He hiding?
And, why is He letting us suffer?
And-- and the-- the sad thing was that more religious the person was, the faster they went into the gas chamber.
They were lined up the first.
You saw men with beards.
They were the first ones lined up and going into the gas chamber.
We saw them.
So this is what was happening all the time.
And there was a uprising one day in the-- in the crematorium.
And we were hide-- a-- a-- a man that was working in the crematorium couldn't take it any longer.
And I guess they blew up part of the crematorium.
There was, toward the very, very end, which was wonderful.
I wish they would have done it before.
And one of them [SIGHS] jumped over the fence.
And they came on over to us.
And we hid them.
I mean, you know, we-- we hid them in-- in our barrack because we had a lot of clothing there that we were supposed to sort.
And it was all the way up to the ceiling.
So we kind of made room for him.
And he was hiding in between.
And they came to us.
And then they said if we know somebody is here, and if you don't tell us who is here, who you hiding, everybody is going to be shot.
So there was only a threat, thank God, because nobody said a word.
But he heard.
This man heard what the German, what this SS told us.
And he came out on his own accord.
He walked out.
What happened to him, I haven't got the slightest idea.
I don't know.
I don't know what happened to him.
Oh, many of them were hung publicly.
We didn't see because we were not on the place where we could see it.
But I have heard from my friends who have survived it that yes.
They were-- there were public hangings out in the open to see what can happen to somebody, definitely, definitely, mhm.
[SIGHS] And we can still smile.
We are here.
We can smile about it.
A lot of things that happened on-- on Brzezinka that-- that were very, very mean.
I mean, it was mean to the point where you thought, why is this happening to us?

And yet we felt very lucky to be there, that all those months that everybody was in the camp, in a very bad camp, like they call it [GERMAN] lager and [GERMAN] lager, and they were a very, very bad lagers.
And those girls, and they were-- and men were suffering.
I mean, they were treated very badly.
And-- and they weren't fed.
And here, we were in a very special place.
But we saw too much.
It was hurting us too much.
And that was maybe more hurtful than-- than being punished bodily.
What kept us going, faith.
I said to my mother, we'll get out of here somehow, somehow.
And after a while when we started to see the molotov, they call it-- at that time, they called molotov cocktails.
It's not.
It wasn't.
The Russians were-- were bombing [INAUDIBLE], I guess, around where we were, not Auschwitz itself, but-- because there were factories around us.
And they start to bomb.
And then we saw them, and we were so happy.
One day, I never forget.
[SIGHS] There was the sirens went on because they-- they were bombing all over.
And I didn't care what is going to happen.
I ran outside, and I looked up in the sky.
And I started to laugh.
And an SS man caught me.
And he took his stick.
And he hit me over my face.
So I had-- I-- I had a line on my face for a long time.
But I was happy to see that somebody is trying to help us, because I knew if somebody is going to help us, must be the Russians.
So that was what was happening, was happening a lot until January 18th.
And what happened then?
[SIGHS] January 18th they told us they're going to line us up.
They didn't tell us what is going to happen.
They lined us up.
And they said, don't take anything with you because there's nothing for you to take with you.
But I-- I says, if you want to take-- it was cold.
So I had a-- I had a men's jacket, like a three quarter length jacket.
And I didn't have a belt.
But I had a rope.
So I tied it around my-- my waist.
And I said that should kind of keep me a little bit warmer then-- then not having anything.
And they lined us up.
And all of a sudden, they said, let's walk.
And while we were walking, I saw the German, the SS men taking books and papers.
And they were throwing it in the-- on-- on the-- and they were burning them.
There was a big pile.
And they were-- they start to burn everything that they could get hold of.
And I said to my mother, this is wonderful.
They're going to take us out of here.
I says, we're going to be liberated now.
I said, they're getting us out of Auschwitz, probably, because they-- look at that, I said.
They're burning all those books and all those papers.
They don't want anybody to know what was happening.
So that was-- that's when we kind of thought that, you know, something is going to happen to us better than Auschwitz.
Little did we know that we were going on a very long journey.
They didn't give us any food.
We went-- we went out of Auschwitz, naturally.
And we start to walk.
And we were walking.
We walked kilometers and kilometers.
And all of a sudden, we start to see dead bodies all over the place, because when they-- somebody couldn't walk anymore, they tied their hands behind them.
They made them bend down.
And they shot them in the back of the head.
So they, you know, they just killed them on the spot.
And I said to my mother, I don't like what I see.
I said there are too many dead bodies all over the place.
And I says, I don't know how far they're going to take us.
And it was January 18th.
And it was snowing.
It was cold.

And it was-- it was-- it was very, very uncomfortable to walk when you didn't have any-- any hose on, just a pair of shoes, hardly any underwear on, and, you know, just a very skinny little dress and a-- and a jacket.
But we had to do it.
And then we were walking and walking.
And then it was night time.
And they took us into a barn.
It was a-- a place where people-- in a small little village.
And they took us into this barn.
And there was an awful lot of-- a lot of straw on one side.
And I said to my mother, you know what I'm going to do?
I says, I'm going to hide in-- in between the straw.
I'm going to bury myself in the straw, until I saw a German come with a pitchfork.
And he was putting the pitchfork into the straw, hoping to find somebody there.
And I said to my mother, I guess this is a bad idea.
So anyway, we slept there.
It was-- we slept there overnight.
And German, and actually, we-- we walked with German shepherds, the German shepherds were-- were watching us just as well as the German soldiers were watching us.
Next day, again, we got up, no food, no nothing.
I went and I kind of brushed the snow aside.
And there was grass underneath.
I picked a few pieces of grass, and I start to eat grass.
And I said to my mother, you know, this is better than nothing.
I say, let's have a little snow.
Let's eat a little snow.
And some grass that we find.
So that's what we did, not a whole lot, just a little, just to have-- get something into our bodies.
And then we-- we were walking again.
We start to walk again.
And we came to a-- another place.
We were very tired.
We stopped because one of the German soldiers told us that their dogs are tired.
So they had to take a rest.
And weak-- and it was very, very, very cold.
We came in to a-- what do you call-- a small-- a small place.
And they had a well where they take water out from, you know?
And it had a ledge that was brick ledge all the way around.
And I said to my mother, I'm awfully tired.
I-- I want-- I want to sit down.
And I-- I just want to sleep, just a little bit while we are waiting.
So I sat down, and my sister sat down.
And I almost dozed off.
And my mother came around.
She start to slap my face.
She says, you can't do it.
You can't do it.
I don't want you to sleep, because if you would be sleeping, you would-- you would freeze to death.
I don't want you to do that.
She kept me up all, you know, while-- while we were sitting there.
She wouldn't let me sleep.
So that went on for a while, about an-- two hours or so.
And then they lined us up again, and we start to walk.
And we saw German ladies from the villages.
They had something underneath their-- their scarves.
They had a shawl.
And they had something on their shawl.
And one lady came close to me, and she had a bread.
And she says to me, take it.
Take it quickly.
Take it.
So I took a piece of bread.
She-- one piece for me, and one to somebody else.
She gave it to somebody else too.
She says, take it.
And I took it.
That saved our lives, because I was able to give a piece to my mother.
And I was able to give a piece to my sister.
It was a small piece of bread.
But nevertheless, it was a piece of bread.
It was-- it was life for us.
That saved our life at that time, I felt.
That-- that gave us a little more strength.
And I think we must have been very close to Ravensbruck.
We were walking.
My mother couldn't walk anymore.
She was so weak.
So my sister and I started to carry her.
And I said, mother, you have to walk a little more.
And well, I was-- so we were dragging her because I didn't want them to come around and tell her if you can't walk, you-- you know you have to kneel down and we'll shoot you right here on the spot.
And just as-- as-- as my mother was not able to walk anymore, when she was so very, very weak, we came to a train station.
And they put us on-- on a train.
And it was a ride maybe a half hour or so.
And we arrived in Ravensbruck.
And Ravensbruck was one of the most horror camps that I found out that it was.
When they took us into Ravensbruck, I-- we-- I never heard of it.
I didn't know where we were.
Then when they walked-- we walked in.
They said, you know where you are?
We said, no.
I said, this is Ravensbruck, and you better behave.
They took us into a barrack.
Again, they gave us a spot for, you know-- the barracks were like bunk beds.
Again, on a very, very bottom of the-- the-- the bunk beds, on earth.
And he says, this is your spot.
And that's where you staying.
They came around.
They wanted that.
And we would [GERMAN] appell.
And they took us out for a standing [GERMAN] appell again, which means, you know, in fives to-- they had to count how many people were there.
And they brought us some food.
And only one-- one dish.
And they gave us a dish for my mother, my sister, and myself, and another girl that was with us.
And as I was holding the dish, a woman ran by me.
She grabbed the dish and ran away with it.
And I found out this woman was a Russian woman.
She was not Jewish.
And she was in the same camp as I was in.
And they must have been very hungry, naturally, like we were.
So she got to the point where she was so hungry that she just ran by and she took my food away.
And let me tell you, from then on, I sure cherish.
If I got a piece of bread, if I ever got a piece of bread, I hid it in under-- under my coat.
I wouldn't let anybody to see it, because they would steal it.
They stole everything from us.
And because we weren't-- they were so hungry, because they must have been long.
I don't know how long they were there.
But there-- they must have been in there a long time.
They were very mean to us.
They hit us.
They beat us.
They kicked us.
They were-- it was just a very horrible camp.
And she says to me, if you're going to say anything to the kapo, she says, you're not going-- they're not going to find you tomorrow morning alive.
So I was afraid to say anything.
Nobody said anything until-- we were there not-- not too long, thank God.
We were there maybe four days.
And after four days, again, they lined us up.
But we are taking you someplace else.
So, again, we started to walk out of the camp.
And I said to my mother, nothing can be worse than this.
I said, wherever we'll go, maybe it's going to be a little bit better.
I say, each time it's better.
It's-- maybe this was better than Auschwitz, maybe not.
But maybe they're going to take us out of Ravensbruck.
Maybe it's going to be a little bit better.
They took us out of Ravensbruck.
We start to walk.
Again, we went to the train station.
And we were walking.
We didn't know we were walking pretty good distance.
They put on train station.
And we ended up in Neustadt-Glewe.
And that was the camp that we were liberated from.
And when we-- when we arrived to Neustadt-Glewe, they, again, they put us into a building.
There was not barrack, like a barrack.
It was a-- a wood-structured building.
And they put us in one room.
There were no bunk beds.
It was just one huge room, huge.
It wasn't huge.
We thought it was big, but it wasn't big because we were like 60 of us in one room.

And the only place, the only way you were able to sleep, if you sat down, and you-- and you pulled up your-- your legs to your chin and sleep that way, because you could not stretch out and go to sleep.
And that was another camp.
It was-- I don't know whether they had-- they didn't have a crematorium there.
But it was not of the best of camps to start with.
We was-- as it happened in-- in-- not too many.
Because the ones that I knew, it was only my mother and my sister and one other person that I knew that ended up, because they selected in-- in Ravensbruck, they selected people randomly.
So I didn't know where the other people went, because they told us this is where you gonna to sleep.
And they taken the other ones in-- in different barracks, in different parts of Ravensbruck.
So I don't know what happened to them from then on.
But I know, you know, when-- when they lined us up, they lined us up with people that were already in Ravensbruck for a while.
Because whoever came with us didn't look familiar to us at all.
It wasn't.
But we were still very lucky to be with my mother and my sister.
So that's-- we ended up in-- in Neustadt-Glewe with my mother and my sister and one other person that I know.
There were-- there were the four of us.
We were liberated--
--from Neustadt-Glewe, we were liberated May 3rd of that year.
When we ended up-- when we got to Neustadt-Glewe, they told us we're going to be working, which was fine.
I felt, you know, working is OK.
So we were all of us.
Well, in the meantime, by the time we went to Neustadt-Glewe, my sister was very weak, lost an awful lot of weight.
My mother was very, very weak, and also, lost a an awful lot of weight.
So they couldn't really put in a good day's work.
And the very first job we had was dig trenches.
And they told us how long and how wide.
And we start to dig trenches.
And it was hard work.
But I-- I was the strongest of-- of us three.
And I dug.
And I finished what I was-- I was supposed to do.
And then I went over and I helped my mother and my sister, because when-- when we went out to dig trenches, there weren't that many SS officers with us.
It was mostly Wehrmacht, you know, from the regular German army.
And I got lucky because one of the Wehrmacht that was watching me dig the trench came over and-- and start to talk to me.
And he says to me, where are you from?
I says, I'm from Czechoslovakia.
He says, oh, I'm from Yugoslavia.
He says, oh, well, then we are not too far from each other.
I said, that's right.
And we started talk, and he befriended me.
And what he did for me, he says to me, tomorrow when you come tomorrow, he says, would you like a baked potato?
I says, I would love it.
I would love it.
So he made a baked potato for me next day.
And I hid it under my coat.
When I-- when-- and when we went back in, in the camp.
Now, that went on for a few days.
After about a week or so, my mother got really very, very weak, and my sister.
And I begged them.
I begged whoever was in charge.
There was a lady who was in charge.
I begged them to leave my-- my mother and my sister behind.
And I'm going to do their work for them.
If they, you know, if I have to dig more trenches, I'll be more than happy to just leave them behind, because they can't walk anymore.
And she was nice enough.
She says, well, you have to promise.
And, you know, you have to prove it that you'll be able to do the work for them.
I says, yeah, I'll prove it.
And they took me out.
And I dug trenches.
And I never stopped all day long, because I had too-- I had to prove it to them that I can do three people's job in one day.
And I did it.
And that went on for a while.
And then all of a sudden, one day, they lined us up.
And they said, we need people to come and work on a railroad station.
I, so, I had no choice, because they selected me, and I went.
And we were like about 25 of us.
And they selected 10 girls.
And they said, you see the-- that-- that railroad car?
Yes.
This is-- they had telephone poles on them.
I said yes.
He says, I want you to take one off.
And I want you to carry it to a certain distance, to a certain place.
And we said, OK.
We didn't know how heavy they were.
But the 10 of us struggled a lot.
But we did-- we did it.
We had to do it.
Otherwise, it would have been the end of us if we couldn't do it.
They had-- they made us do it.
And we did it.
And that was that only-- that was one day when we had to do that.
And then the following day, we went out to the railroad station again.
And by that time, my sister was really very, very sick.
She was in a very bad shape.
So I asked this-- a German soldier.
I says, please do me a favor.
I said instead of just giving me a baked potato, I says, bake this potato until it's black.
Make coal out of it.
He said, why?
I says, because I need it.
So he did.
And he gave it to me.
And I put it under my-- my jacket.
It wasn't that big.
It was a small piece.
I put it under my-- because my-- my jacket, and I took it back into the camp.
I smuggled it because they were watching us was very, very closely, so we don't bring any food or anything from the outside.
And I broke it up into little pieces.
And I said to my sister, I want you to eat this.
And I said to my mother, I want you to eat some of this too, I said, because it's going to help your stomach, because they had-- they had dysentery.
They were running an awful lot.
I says, I want you to eat it.
And it went on for two days.
And they got a little bit better.
I didn't know.
It was just-- I just took it out of my, I don't know.
I just-- just a trial and-- and-- and-- and luck.
And when-- when we finished working on the railroad, they took us on a farm.
And on a farm, we, again, we start to dig-- we have to dig more trenches.
And as we were digging trenches, I saw a little girl in a not too distant, like around fence.
She was not too far from me.
She must have been maybe five or six years old.
And I went over to her to talk to her where-- I mean, I wasn't supposed to, but I was so hungry.
She was eating carrots.
And I went over.
And I said to her, would you like to give me a piece of carrot?
She says to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And she ran away from me.
She would not share a carrot.
This is how I knew that all, even the children in Germany knew that we were Jews.
OK, we're going to pause here.
This is the end of tape three.
